one_line_takeaway: Across 240 LLM-generated Rust AEAD samples, only 23.3% compiled and 57.1% of those compiled samples still contained cryptographic misuse (nonce reuse, hardcoded keys, pervasive unsafe unwrap() error handling) that CodeQL's general-purpose static analysis entirely missed, achieving a 0% true-positive / 100% false-positive rate versus the authors' purpose-built crypto rule-based analyzer.
key_finding: Only 56/240 (23.3%, 95% CI [18.4%,29.1%]) of generated samples compiled. Compilation success did not differ significantly by model (DeepSeek Coder 25.0% 20/80, GPT-4o 22.5% 18/80, Gemini 2.5 Pro 22.5% 18/80; chi2=0.19, df=2, p=0.911, V=0.028) but differed sharply by prompt strategy (Zero-shot 35.0% 21/60, Security-focused 28.3% 17/60, Constraint-based 23.3% 14/60, Chain-of-thought 6.7% 4/60; chi2=14.72, df=3, p=0.002, V=0.248) and by algorithm (AES-256-GCM 34.2% vs ChaCha20-Poly1305 12.5%, a 2.7x gap; chi2=14.56, p<0.001, V=0.246). A strong model-algorithm interaction emerged: GPT-4o and DeepSeek Coder compiled AES-256-GCM exclusively (0/60 ChaCha20 successes each) while Gemini 2.5 Pro favored ChaCha20-Poly1305 (15/60) over AES (3/60). GPT-4o with the security-focused prompt for AES-256-GCM hit 100% compilation (10/10) in two independent runs. Of the 184 non-compiling samples, error causes were API hallucinations 41.3% (e.g., calls to nonexistent generate_nonce()/Nonce::generate()), type errors 28.6%, trait errors 18.5% (commonly aes_gcm::Error not satisfying std::error::Error with the ? operator), unresolved imports 11.6%; 82.1% of chain-of-thought failures specifically involved API hallucination. Among the 56 compiled samples, CodeQL flagged only 2 findings (both in one GPT-4o/chain-of-thought sample, both 'hardcoded cryptographic value' warnings on the idiomatic zero-init-then-fill nonce pattern) -- both manually confirmed false positives, giving CodeQL 0% true-positive rate and 100% false-positive rate across the dataset. The authors' rule-based analyzer found vulnerabilities in 32/56 compiled samples (57.1%, 95% CI [44.2%,69.4%]), with 2/32 rated critical (3.6%, 95% CI [1.0%,12.1%]): one multi-call nonce reuse (CWE-329, ChaCha20-Poly1305, chain-of-thought) and one hardcoded secret using a literal byte-string key (CWE-798, ChaCha20-Poly1305, zero-shot), both from Gemini 2.5 Pro output. Unsafe error handling via unwrap()/expect() on crypto operations (CWE-252, medium severity) affected 32/56 (57.1%) compiled samples across all three models; DeepSeek Coder's compiled samples avoided it entirely. By model among compiled samples: Gemini 2.5 Pro 2/18 critical (11.1%), GPT-4o 0/18 critical (0.0%, but 2 CodeQL false positives), DeepSeek Coder 0/20 critical (0.0%, but its compiled set was confounded, coming exclusively from AES zero-shot/constraint-based configurations).
core_contribution: The authors' own contribution (distinct from the Related Work in Section 2, which surveys Pearce et al.'s Copilot study, Perry et al.'s AI-assistant user study, Kang et al. on LLM exploit chaining, Egele et al.'s Android crypto-misuse survey, Lazar et al.'s CVE case study, CryptoGuard/CogniCrypt, and Umer's MISRA C++ study) is: (1) a controlled empirical study generating 240 Rust code samples (3 LLMs x 2 AEAD algorithms x 4 prompt strategies x 10 samples) for AES-256-GCM and ChaCha20-Poly1305; (2) a four-stage evaluation pipeline (generation, Clippy compilation check, CodeQL static analysis with the rust-security-extended.qls suite, and a custom rule-based crypto-specific analyzer); (3) the rule-based crypto-specific analyzer itself, covering nine regex/structural rules mapped to seven CWEs, including provenance tracking to avoid flagging the idiomatic zero-init-then-fill nonce pattern as hardcoded, and brace-depth loop-body extraction for nonce-reuse-in-loop detection; (4) statistically grounded findings that prompt strategy and algorithm choice significantly affect compilation success while model choice does not, and that chain-of-thought prompting is harmful for cryptographic code generation.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the traditional sense; the paper's security concern is developer-facing: LLMs used to generate cryptographic (AEAD) code may silently produce implementations with semantic security flaws (nonce reuse, hardcoded keys/nonces, weak randomness, unsafe error handling, deprecated APIs, key derived from untrusted external input without a KDF) that compile and run without error yet violate AEAD's core requirement of nonce uniqueness under a fixed key. The downstream risk cited is standard AEAD misuse consequences (authentication-key recovery and ciphertext forgery under nonce reuse in GCM, per Joux's two-time-pad analysis) and denial-of-service via unwrap()/expect() panics on attacker-controlled malformed ciphertext.
limitations: Explicitly stated by the authors: the crypto-specific analyzer was validated on only 26 total test cases (6 synthetic + 20 translated CryptoAPI-Bench cases) plus manual verification of the 56 real compiled samples, so novel Rust patterns outside this validation set could yield undetected vulnerabilities; the analyzer's single-file regex-based design has documented blind spots on cross-module data flow and indirect initialization (e.g., keys/IVs reaching code via static references, as_bytes()-resolved &str, or Vec-built buffers), evidenced by 4/18 false negatives in the CryptoAPI-Bench validation. DeepSeek Coder's zero-critical-vulnerability result is confounded because its entire compiled sample set came from only AES zero-shot/constraint-based configurations, making cross-model security comparison unreliable for that model. CodeQL conclusions are specific to the rust-security-extended.qls suite; custom AEAD-aware queries might change results, and the false-positive classification, while manually verified by the authors, was not independently checked by a third party. The study covers only AEAD (AES-256-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) and not key exchange, signatures, or hashing, and is specific to Rust's type system and crates -- results may not generalize to other languages or crypto primitives. With n=10 samples per configuration, statistical power is adequate only for medium-to-large effects (Cramer's V >= 0.25); the model-algorithm interaction is flagged by the authors as exploratory and needing confirmation with larger samples. Multiple chi-square tests were run without Bonferroni correction (justified by the authors as an exploratory study, with all p-values/effect sizes reported for reader interpretation).

datasets_used: 240 self-generated Rust code samples (3 LLMs x 2 AEAD algorithms x 4 prompt strategies x 10 samples)
CryptoAPI-Bench (20 cases translated from Java to Rust for analyzer validation)
6 synthetic Rust snippets authored by the researchers for analyzer validation
quantitative_results: Compilation by model: DeepSeek Coder 25.0% (20/80), GPT-4o 22.5% (18/80), Gemini 2.5 Pro 22.5% (18/80); chi2=0.19, df=2, p=0.911, Cramer's V=0.028
Compilation by prompt: Zero-shot 35.0% (21/60), Security-focused 28.3% (17/60), Constraint-based 23.3% (14/60), Chain-of-thought 6.7% (4/60); chi2=14.72, df=3, p=0.002, V=0.248
Model x algorithm interaction (compiled/total): GPT-4o 18/60 AES vs 0/60 ChaCha; DeepSeek 20/60 AES vs 0/60 ChaCha; Gemini 3/60 AES vs 15/60 ChaCha
GPT-4o security-focused AES-256-GCM: 100% compilation (10/10), reproduced across two independent runs
Compilation error taxonomy (of 184 failures): API Hallucinations 41.3%, Type Errors 28.6%, Trait Errors 18.5%, Unresolved Imports 11.6%
82.1% of chain-of-thought failures specifically involved invoking non-existent APIs
CodeQL: 2 findings total across 56 compiled samples, both false positives -> 0% true-positive rate, 100% false-positive rate
Rule-based analyzer: vulnerabilities in 32/56 compiled samples = 57.1%, 95% CI [44.2%, 69.4%]
Critical vulnerabilities: 2/32 = 3.6% of compiled samples, 95% CI [1.0%, 12.1%] (1 multi-call nonce reuse CWE-329, 1 hardcoded secret CWE-798)
Unsafe error handling (CWE-252): 32/56 = 57.1% of compiled samples, 95% CI [44.1%, 69.2%]
Security findings by model (compiled only): Gemini 2.5 Pro 2/18 critical (11.1%), GPT-4o 0/18 critical (0.0%, 2 CodeQL false positives), DeepSeek Coder 0/20 critical (0.0%)
Analyzer validation on CryptoAPI-Bench (n=20): 100% precision (14 TP, 0 FP), 78% recall (14/18), 88% F1, 80% accuracy; per-CWE recall CWE-330 6/6, CWE-329 4/6, CWE-798 4/6
baselines_compared: CodeQL static analysis using the rust-security-extended.qls query suite (38 queries) -- general-purpose baseline compared against the authors' rule-based crypto-specific analyzer
Four prompt strategies compared against each other as experimental baselines: Zero-shot (reference baseline), Constraint-based, Chain-of-thought, and the authors' Security-focused prompt
